title: Neomammillaria parkinsonii
taxonomy: Neomammillaria parkinsonii is now classified as Mammillaria parkinsonii, which is a species of cactus in the family Cactaceae. It belongs to the genus Mammillaria, which consists of over 200 species of small to medium-sized cacti. The species is native to Mexico and is often found growing on rocky outcrops and scrublands. Mammillaria parkinsonii is characterized by its small size, typically reaching heights of 2-5 cm, and its dense, white spines. The plant's flowers are also small and white, appearing in the spring and summer months.
uses: Neomammillaria parkinsonii, also known as the Parkinson's pine cactus, has been traditionally used in Mexican folk medicine. The plant's extracts are said to have antibacterial and antifungal properties, which can aid in wound healing. Additionally, the plant's sap is used to treat skin conditions such as eczema and acne. The antibacterial properties of Neomammillaria parkinsonii may also help prevent infections in minor cuts and scrapes. Further research is needed to confirm the plant's full range of medicinal properties.

intro: Neomammillaria parkinsonii, also known as the "Pincushion Cactus" or "Pincushion Mammillaria," is a small, globular cactus species native to Mexico and the southwestern United States. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory and antiseptic effects, and has been used to treat various health conditions such as wounds, skin infections, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Neomammillaria parkinsonii include flavonoids, alkaloids, and glycosides, which have been shown to possess antioxidant and antimicrobial properties. Traditionally, the plant is prepared as an infusion or decoction, and is often used in herbal remedies such as teas, ointments, and salves to promote wound healing and reduce inflammation.
